summary: On Wednesday, May 13, 2026, Kouri Richins, the Utah mother and author who wrote a children’s book about grief after her husband's death, was sentenced to life in prison without the possibility of parole. The sentencing took place in a Park City courtroom on what would have been her late husband Eric Richins’ 44th birthday.

Richins, 35, was found guilty in March 2026 of aggravated murder for lacing her husband’s Moscow Mule cocktail with five times the lethal dose of fentanyl in March 2022. She was also convicted of attempted murder for trying to poison him weeks earlier on Valentine’s Day with a fentanyl-laced sandwich.

Prosecutors successfully argued that Richins was over $4 million in debt and sought to kill her husband to collect millions in life insurance and inherit his estate. Text messages presented during the trial showed she was also planning a future with another man at the time of the murder.

In a poignant moment, Eric’s father, Eugene Richins, told the court that the life sentence was necessary to ensure his three grandsons never have to fear the person who killed their father. Social workers also read statements from the three boys—now ages 9, 11, and 13—who expressed that they would feel unsafe if their mother were ever released.

Despite the verdict, Kouri Richins maintained her innocence during a 30-minute statement, calling the conviction "an absolute lie." Her legal team, which rested its case without calling a single witness, has announced plans to appeal both the conviction and the life sentence.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Warsh's 54-45 confirmation as a threat to Fed independence, emphasizing "Trump pick" and "puppet" rhetoric with alarmist tones like "most divisive" vote ever, while right-leaning ones portray it as a "big win" realizing "Trump's economic vision" through triumphant "regime change" language critiquing Powell's inflation legacy.
Pivotal divides emerge on Powell's tenure—right highlights it as "too late," left de-emphasizes—and Fetterman's lone Democratic vote, touted rightward as broad appeal.
Center coverage neutrally stresses a "fraught moment" amid inflation above 2%.
All agree on the procedural outcome and Trump's nomination, underscoring ideological clashes over autonomy versus reform.
summary: On Wednesday, May 13, 2026, the U.S. Senate confirmed Kevin Warsh as the next Chairman of the Federal Reserve, a move that signals a potential "regime change" for American monetary policy under the Trump administration.

The Senate voted 54-45 to install Warsh as chair. The confirmation was largely partisan, with all Republicans present voting in favor and Senator John Fetterman serving as the lone Democrat to cross party lines to support the nomination.

Warsh’s elevation was finalized through two separate votes. On Tuesday, he was confirmed to a full 14-year term on the Fed’s Board of Governors. Wednesday's vote specifically appointed him to the four-year term as Chairman.

Warsh will officially take the gavel on May 14, 2026, when Jerome Powell’s term as chair expires. However, in a break with 75 years of precedent, Powell has announced he will remain on the Board of Governors as a regular member until at least 2028 to oversee the conclusion of a DOJ investigation into Fed headquarters renovations.

Known historically as an "inflation hawk," Warsh recently signaled an openness to the President's calls for lower interest rates and a reduction of the Fed's "bloated" balance sheet. He begins his term as inflation sits at 3.8% and amid intense pressure from the White House to spur economic activity.
title: Kevin Warsh wins Senate confirmation as the next Federal Reserve chair

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the supplier as a "drug counselor" haunted by "mistakes," humanizing him with remorseful tones and empathy for the "Friends' star" victim, while right-leaning ones portray a profit-driven "drug middleman" delivering "fatal ketamine doses" who "learns his fate" through "jailed" justice, emphasizing culpability.
Center coverage neutrally highlights procedural sentencing in an "overdose case.
Pivotal divides emerge on remorse statements and ketamine quantities like 25 vials, with right spotlighting the "Ketamine Queen’s" 15-year term.
All agree on the two-year prison sentence and Perry’s death, underscoring rehab-versus-punishment ideological rifts in drug narratives.
summary: On Wednesday, Judge Sherilyn Peace Garnett sentenced 56-year-old Erik Fleming to two years in prison for delivering ketamine that killed Matthew Perry , making him the fourth defendant sentenced in the case.

After Perry sought more ketamine than doctors would provide for depression treatment, a friend introduced him to Fleming, who connected the actor to Jasveen Sangha, a dealer prosecutors called 'The Ketamine Queen,' sentenced last month to 15 years.

Court records show Fleming delivered 25 vials for $6,000 four days before Perry's death on Oct. 28, 2023; his early cooperation—pleading guilty in August 2024 before arrests were announced—reduced his potential four-year sentence despite prosecutors arguing his drug-counselor role selling to someone with documented addiction should count against him.

Before sentencing, Fleming expressed remorse, telling the judge his actions were 'a nightmare I can't wake up from' and his great remorse 'can't compare to the agony' caused to Perry's family. Defense lawyers had sought three months in prison and nine months of treatment.

Kenneth Iwamasa, Perry's live-in assistant who injected the fatal dose, remains the final defendant awaiting sentencing in two weeks. Perry, who died at 54 as Chandler Bing on 'Friends,' will be memorialized through his namesake foundation receiving auction proceeds from the show's memorabilia.
title: Drug counselor who sold Matthew Perry fatal dose of ketamine is sentenced to two years in prison

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the unanimous overturn as a mere technicality from "jury interference," embedding presumption of guilt via "disgraced" and "infamous" labels while foregrounding victims' "killings," evoking skeptical, victim-centric tones; right-leaning ones dramatize it as a "shockingly overturned" "wild twist" and "jury tampering scandal," highlighting judicial vindication with emphatic "unanimously.
Pivotal divides emerge on clerk misconduct—right amplifies as trial poison, left de-emphasizes—and Murdaugh's 40-year financial sentence, left touting containment, right noting "massive break.
Center coverage stays dispassionate with plain "overturns.

summary: On Wednesday, the South Carolina Supreme Court overturned Alex Murdaugh 's murder convictions and ordered a new trial, citing "improper external influences on the jury" by former Colleton County Clerk Becky Hill in a unanimous 5-0 decision.

Evidence cited by the court indicated Hill urged jurors to "watch his body language" and "Don't let the defense confuse you," marring the 2023 trial that convicted Murdaugh of killing his wife, Maggie, and 22-year-old son, Paul.

Following her resignation in March 2024, Hill pleaded guilty in December 2025 to perjury and obstruction of justice charges for using her court position to promote a book about the trial, receiving three years of probation.

Despite the vacated murder convictions, the 57-year-old Murdaugh will remain imprisoned while serving concurrent state and federal sentences totaling 40 years for dozens of financial crimes, keeping him incarcerated for decades.

South Carolina Attorney General Alan Wilson must now decide whether to retry Murdaugh for the 2021 killings of Maggie and Paul in circuit court, determining the next phase of the sprawling legal saga.
title: South Carolina Supreme Court grants Alex Murdaugh's appeal for new trial

summary: On Thursday, President Donald Trump met Chinese President Xi Jinping in Beijing for a bilateral summit, marking Trump's first visit to China in 9 years.

Ahead of the summit, the Embassy reiterated its position on 'four red lines' in China-US relations: 'The Taiwan Question', 'Democracy and Human Rights', 'Paths and Political Systems' and 'China's Development Right'.
Accompanied by Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Secretary of War Pete Hegseth, Trump received a ceremonial welcome at the Great Hall from the Liberation Army Honour Guard Battalion.
Discussions include Iran, trade, and technology, as Jinping stated, 'I look forward to our discussions on major issues important to our two countries and the world.'
Trump affirmed both nations will 'have a fantastic future together' and noted, 'We've had a fantastic relationship,' framing the summit as foundation for strengthened China-US ties.
title: Trump arrives in Beijing for talks with China’s Xi on Iran war, trade and US arms sales to Taiwan

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Trump's remarks as "platitudes," contrasting them with Xi's sobering "warns" of "conflict" over Taiwan mishandling, emphasizing U.S. diplomatic superficiality amid divisions.

summary: On Thursday in Beijing, Chinese President Xi Jinping warned Donald Trump that Taiwan could push the nations into "conflict" if mishandled, marking a stark opening to their summit at the Great Hall.
Relations between Washington and Beijing have stabilized since the two presidents met last October in Busan, South Korea, following years of diplomatic engagement at multilateral summits.
Reuters reported that negotiators are weighing a framework to ease tariffs on $30 billion in goods, while Trump stated his priority involves asking the Chinese President to "open up" China.
Rubio indicated the US would push China to help conclude the war in Iran, urging an active role in the Persian Gulf due to Asia 's heavy energy dependence on the region.
Trump will meet the Chinese President again on Friday for a photo session and lunch, stating he hopes for a "fantastic future" for the world's two largest economies, the USA and China.
title: Xi Jinping Warns Donald Trump That Taiwan Disputes Could Lead to U.S.-China Conflict, Chinese State Media Says

summary: On Wednesday, Speaker Mike Johnson and Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries announced a bipartisan task force to review how the House handles sexual misconduct claims, with both leaders expressing full support for improving reporting mechanisms.
The initiative follows the resignations last month of Democratic Rep. Eric Swalwell and Republican Rep. Tony Gonzales following sexual misconduct allegations, intensifying calls for reform.

Democratic Rep. Teresa Leger Fernandez and Republican Rep. Kat Cammack have been designated to lead the effort, with Cammack noting the group will address the "convoluted process" that discourages staffers from reporting misconduct.

House Administration Chair Bryan Steil and ranking member Joseph D. Morelle will participate in an ex officio capacity, helping the group "identify reforms and solutions to make Congress a safer work environment for women and all survivors."

Cammack said Congress must "set the standard" for workplace protections, emphasizing that accountability requires "prevention, transparency, education, and systems people can actually trust" as lawmakers prepare for coming months of reform work.
title: House set to launch bipartisan push to combat sexual misconduct in Congress

summary: On Thursday, May 14, 2026, the US Commerce Department cleared around 10 Chinese firms to purchase Nvidia H200 AI chips, with CEO Jensen Huang joining President Donald Trump in Beijing for the announcement.

Before US export curbs tightened, Nvidia commanded about 95% of China's advanced chip market, though Beijing now fears imports could undermine a strategic push to develop homegrown artificial intelligence technology.

Although cleared for purchase, not a single delivery has occurred as Chinese firms pulled back following guidance from Beijing, while Trump negotiated a structure requiring the US to receive 25% of the revenue from these chip sales.

US rules issued in January require buyers to demonstrate "sufficient security procedures" to prevent military use, and Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick noted that Beijing prioritizes investment in their own domestic industry.

Tech rivalry continues to snarl trade, leaving the world's most valuable chipmaker caught between conflicting national priorities; analysts expect the summit to act as a tactical catalyst for Chinese equities.
title: US clears H200 chip sales to 10 China firms as Nvidia CEO looks for breakthrough: Reuters

The session aims to redraw congressional districts, particularly making the sixth district more competitive.
With one legislative day remaining, a compromise on redistricting seems unlikely before the regular session ends.
title: McMaster Set to Call South Carolina Special Session on Redistricting: Reports

summary: Mississippi Governor Tate Reeves canceled a special session to consider new voting maps for the state's three Supreme Court voting districts following a U.S. Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals decision that vacated a prior ruling requiring redistricting.
The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that Louisiana's congressional district map was an unconstitutional racial gerrymander, impacting provisions of the federal Voting Rights Act.
Reeves stated that lawmakers can redraw Mississippi's judicial map only during the next regular session in January 2027 to avoid affecting upcoming elections.
title: Mississippi governor cancels redistricting special session

summary: A new Quantus Insights poll released Wednesday shows former Navy SEAL Ed Gallrein leading incumbent Representative Thomas Massie 48.3% to 43.1% in Kentucky's 4th Congressional District GOP primary.
President Donald Trump endorsed Gallrein, fueling the race after clashing repeatedly with Massie over federal spending and the Epstein Files Transparency Act.
The contest has become the most expensive House primary in history, with more than $25 million in ad spending, as Gallrein gains ground with women at 52.3%.
Kentucky voters began early, in-person voting on Thursday ahead of the May 19 primary, which tests Trump's endorsement power against Republicans who have broken with him.
Quantus Insights described Massie's 43% support as a "warning sign," noting that a meaningful share of the Republican primary electorate is prepared to move in another direction.
title: New Poll Shows Gallrein Overtaking Massie in GOP Primary

Right-Leaning coverage portrays it positively as a patriotic "match" to the nation's 250th anniversary, employing neutral phrases like "eyes" and "hand out" to highlight celebratory intent without skepticism.

summary: President Donald Trump is considering issuing 250 presidential pardons to celebrate the 250th anniversary of America, according to The Wall Street Journal. The president may announce the acts of mercy on July 4, 2026, or his 80th birthday on June 14th.

Trump's expanded use of presidential clemency has driven record pardon requests, with more than 16,000 formal submissions last year—the highest since 1937. On his first day back in office last year, Trump issued pardons to over 1,500 individuals involved in the Capitol storming.

High-Profile figures seeking a reprieve include Ghislaine Maxwell and Sam Bankman-Fried, per The Wall Street Journal. Maxwell's lawyer floated clemency in exchange for testimony, while Trump has publicly scoffed at a pardon for the FTX founder.

White House officials have raised concerns that issuing too many pardons could affect Republicans in the midterm elections this fall. Officials noted "no decisions had been made," emphasizing that Trump is the ultimate decision maker on clemency actions.

Trump has also brought up "widespread pardons for his staff before he leaves office," suggesting the 250-pardon plan may be part of a broader clemency strategy. Critics argue such moves risk "normalizing public corruption" by downplaying crimes committed by officials.
title: White House Explores 250 Pardons to Mark America’s 250th Birthday : WSJ

summary: On Wednesday, the Pentagon launched the "Low-Cost Containerized Missiles" program, establishing framework agreements with Anduril, CoAspire, Leidos, and Zone 5 to acquire over 10,000 cruise missiles over three years starting in 2027.

Seeking "cheap mass" for conflicts like the war in Iran, the Defense Department aims to expand the industrial base beyond traditional prime contractors and scale affordable strike capabilities rapidly.

Purchasing test missiles from all four vendors begins in June 2026, while Leidos will provide an "initial" 3,000 LCCMs modeled after its AGM-190A Small Cruise Missile but approximately twice the size.
A "parallel" agreement with defense startup Castelion establishes a two-year contract for at least 500 Blackbeard hypersonic weapons annually, with an option to extend for up to five years.
title: Pentagon reaches agreements with defense firms on containerized missiles

summary: On Wednesday, Rich Danker, chief spokesperson for Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., resigned in protest of the administration's new policy allowing flavored vape sales, following FDA Commissioner Marty Makary's resignation on Tuesday.
Last week, the FDA issued new guidance allowing fruit- and candy-flavored vape products on store shelves, reversing previous bans despite evidence linking them to youth vaping.

Danker warned in his resignation letter that authorizing flavored e-cigarettes "would appeal to children and expose them to nicotine addiction, lung damage, and higher risk of cancer." He cited senior HHS officials seeking approval despite known health risks.
title: RFK Jr.’s chief HHS spokesperson resigns over flavored vapes

summary: On Tuesday, Secretary of State Marco Rubio traveled to Beijing with President Donald Trump for high-stakes talks with Chinese President Xi Jinping, with a viral Air Force One photo capturing the delegation's arrival.

China previously sanctioned Rubio in 2020 after he criticized Beijing regarding Xinjiang, Uyghurs, and Hong Kong while serving as a senator, measures that technically banned the Secretary from entering the country.

To bypass the entry ban, Chinese officials reportedly used an alternate Mandarin transliteration for Rubio's surname; diplomats told Agence France-Presse this linguistic switch allowed Beijing to technically sidestep sanctions attached to the older spelling.

Chinese Embassy spokesperson Liu Pengyu told Agence France-Presse that Beijing would not block the Secretary's travel, stating, "The sanctions target Mr. Rubio's words and deeds when he served as a US senator concerning China."

Rubio told reporters last week that Taiwan and the Taiwan Strait are likely to come up during talks, noting both countries understand that destabilization in the Indo-Pacific would serve neither side's interests.
title: China tweaks Rubio’s name to bypass travel ban for Trump-Xi meeting

summary: On Wednesday, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention reported about 70,000 Americans died of drug overdoses last year, a 14% decrease marking the third straight annual decline.

Increased naloxone availability, expanded addiction treatment, and opioid lawsuit settlement money fueled the decline, while regulatory changes in China a few years ago reduced precursor chemicals for fentanyl.
Brown University researcher Brandon Marshall said he is "cautiously optimistic" about the trend, though he warned deaths could rise rapidly if policy changes or drug supply shifts occur.

The Center for Forensic Science Research and Education identified 23 new drugs in the first five months of 2026, including cychlorphine, a synthetic opioid toxicology expert Alex Krotulski described as up to 10 times stronger than fentanyl.
Kimberly Douglas of Black Moms Against Overdose criticized the Trump administration's decision to stop funding test strips and kits, calling them essential harm reduction services.
title: Fatal drug overdoses fall for third straight year: CDC

summary: On Tuesday, billionaire Stephen Cloobeck was arrested in West Hollywood on an outstanding felony warrant for alleged witness intimidation, posting $300,000 bond before his release.
Reports indicate the arrest relates to an ongoing criminal case against Cloobeck's fiancée, Adva Lavie, whom he allegedly attempted to shield by preventing three victims from testifying.
The California Post reported that Cloobeck allegedly threatened a victim's attorney, while Lavie, also known as Mia Ventura, faces six felony charges including residential burglary and grand theft.
Previously a major backer of Congressman Eric Swalwell , Cloobeck recently withdrew financial support following sexual misconduct allegations that led to Swalwell's resignation from Congress.
title: Ex-Democratic California gubernatorial candidate Stephen Cloobeck arrested in L.A. County

summary: Department of Homeland Security officials confirmed that Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents may assist with perimeter security at the 2026 FIFA World Cup, but will not screen spectators for immigration status.

Earlier concerns regarding potential immigration enforcement raids persisted after advocacy groups argued that past ICE operations at public events intimidated travelers and discouraged attendance at venues including airports and military ceremonies.

Miami host committee co-chair Rdney Barreto received guarantees that ICE will not operate inside stadiums, stating this will not turn into some "round them up" type of thing. That's not the purpose of this.

Los Angeles officials initially indicated federal agents would assist with local security operations but later retracted that statement, reflecting coordination challenges between federal agencies and host cities during tournament planning.
title: ICE could target World Cup matches in US
topic_full: Immigration and Customs Enforcement · United States

summary: The Senate voted unanimously to move forward on a resolution that would withhold senators' pay during future government shutdowns.
The resolution, proposed by Sen. John Kennedy, would require the Senate secretary to withhold compensation for senators during a shutdown, taking effect after the 2026 midterm elections.
Frustration with government shutdowns has been growing, with lawmakers on both sides previously introducing bills to withhold their pay and allow federal workers to be paid during lapses in funding.
title: Senate unanimously advances resolution suspending senators’ pay during shutdown

summary: Trump Mobile confirmed that it will begin shipping its 'T1' phones this week to customers who pre-ordered and paid deposits last year.
The T1 phone is advertised to have features like a 6.78-inch AMOLED screen, 50-megapixel cameras, 5,000-mAh battery, fingerprint and face unlock, Snapdragon chipset, and Android OS.
While Trump Mobile initially claimed the T1 would be made in the US, it has since dropped that claim and now says it is 'designed with American values in mind.
title: Trump Mobile says T1 Phones will begin shipping this week

summary: On Tuesday, Louisiana Sen. John Kennedy rejected a viral screenshot shared by President Donald Trump on Truth Social, confirming the quote attributed to him was a fabrication.

Originally posted by a user named JOSH DUNLAP, the fake claim demanded former President Barack Obama return $120 million earned through "taxpayer-generated prestige", threatening a Department of Justice referral within three days.

Lead Stories traced the quote to a "satire" website, confirming the $120 million narrative has been recycled multiple times and previously falsely attributed to figures like Kash Patel and Vince Gill.
When questioned by NOTUS, Kennedy denied the statements, expressing confusion and stating, "Somebody told me there was something floating around on the internet" about him accusing Obama.
Such recycled fabrications demonstrate how misinformation persists on social media, gaining traction among conservative audiences despite lacking any factual basis.
title: Trump shares fake Sen. Kennedy quote about Obama

summary: President Donald Trump demanded Wednesday that congressional aide Robert Karem be fired for making Republican Senator Mitch McConnell "look foolish and completely out of it" during a Senate Appropriations Committee hearing.

During Tuesday's hearing, McConnell attempted to conclude proceedings while Senators Tammy Baldwin, Jeanne Shaheen, and John Kennedy still had questions; Karem intervened to ensure they could participate.

Trump defended McConnell on Truth Social, claiming the 84-year-old leader was not confused but unaware why he was being interrupted. He accused Karem of "grandstanding" and having "tremendous" support from Democrats.
Journalists noted that Karem is a respected Capitol Hill national security staffer who served as a Department of Defense appointee under Trump, contradicting the president's "Never Trumper" label.
title: Trump demands GOP staffer be fired for making McConnell look ‘out of it’

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the proceedings as a "rape retrial" starring a "disgraced mogul" who merely "complains" of chest pain, amplifying #MeToo accountability and allegations from nearly 100 women with unsympathetic tones that presume guilt.

Right-Leaning outlets counter by labeling it a "sex crimes trial" disrupted by a "health scare," humanizing the 74-year-old defendant through sympathetic portrayals that de-emphasize rape charges and question prosecution intensity.
Center-Leaning reports neutrally note "reports chest pain" in procedural terms.

Pivotal divides emerge in health incident interpretations and #MeToo ties, yet all agree deliberations began Wednesday and ended early due to pains, underscoring clashes between victim justice and due process priorities.
summary: On Wednesday, jurors began deliberating in the rape retrial of Harvey Weinstein , weighing a Manhattan case involving Jessica Mann that epitomized the #MeToo movement.
This second retrial follows a proceeding last year where jurors failed to reach a verdict, after an appeals court overturned Weinstein's 2020 New York conviction.
The current jury heard nearly three weeks of testimony, including five days from Mann, who testified that Weinstein subjected her to unwanted sex on March 18, 2013.
Lawyers for Weinstein, 74, maintain the encounter was consensual, while Mann has said she was "normalizing everything" regarding her complicated feelings.
Oscar-Winning Weinstein faces one count of rape in the third degree, following criminal convictions in New York and California that were generated by other accusations.
title: Harvey Weinstein reports chest pain and court ends early as jury deliberates in his rape retrial

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the Epstein testimony as elite impunity, emphasizing "billionaire" privilege, "rich remain protected," and a victim's emotional "breaks down" over DOJ exposing her name "500+ times.
Right-Leaning coverage highlights justice system horrors, portraying "tearfully testified" ordeals at the House Oversight hearing.
Center-Leaning reports neutrally stress "abused" claims to lawmakers, de-emphasizing partisan angles.
Pivotal divides emerge on government complicity versus institutional scrutiny.
All converge on core abuse under house arrest, revealing ideological rifts between class antagonism and accountability failures.
summary: On Tuesday, a survivor identified as Roza testified before the House Oversight Committee in Florida, detailing how Jeffrey Epstein sexually abused her while he was under house arrest.
Recruited from Uzbekistan in 2008 by modeling agent Jean-Luc Brunel with promises of a career, Roza was coerced into a situation where she met Epstein for work.
While serving a county jail sentence that allowed 16 hours of daily freedom, Epstein subjected her to abuse for three years; Roza testified this "made justice feel impossible."
During the hearing, Roza attacked the Department of Justice for leaving her name unredacted in public files, claiming the agency protected "the rich and powerful" while exposing victims.
Democratic lawmakers organized the hearing to keep the case in the spotlight, citing reports that a 2008 plea deal enabled Epstein to continue his abuse for almost another decade.
title: Epstein Survivor Roza Tells House Hearing He Abused Her Under House Arrest

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Brett Ratner as "controversial" who "tags along" amid Melania's absence "sparking questions," employing ironic mockery and negative tones to imply scandalous entourage choices, while right-leaning coverage portrays him as an "entertainment heavyweight" joining Trump with "top business leaders" like Elon Musk and Tim Cook—totaling trillion-dollar net worth—for purposeful "scouting," using upbeat, promotional language to celebrate deal-making prowess.
Pivotal divides emerge over Ratner's past accusations and career "comeback" tied to Trump, which left emphasizes negatively and right de-emphasizes positively.
Center coverage neutrally highlights scouting sites, differing by avoiding judgment.
Both agree on Ratner's Air Force One travel for 'Rush Hour 4' locations, underscoring ideological splits on elite associations versus personal scandals.
summary: Director Brett Ratner joined President Donald Trump 's delegation to China on Tuesday, boarding Air Force One to scout filming locations for the upcoming movie Rush Hour 4 during the three-day trip to Beijing.

President Trump has long championed a revival of the buddy-cop franchise, which earned more than $850 million globally across three releases, and reportedly urged Paramount Pictures to develop the sequel intended to film largely in China.

Ratner's career stalled in 2017 after multiple women accused him of sexual misconduct, including model Natasha Henstridge; he recently returned to directing with the Amazon-funded documentary Melania, his first feature since the 2014 movie Hercules.
Joining the delegation are business leaders including Tesla's Elon Musk and Apple's Tim Cook, though a White House official did not clarify Ratner's official status within the group.
title: ‘Melania’ Director to Scout ‘Rush Hour 4’ Sites While with Trump in China

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes legal consequences like a potential three-year sentence and precise incident dates, framing actions with phrases like "repeatedly crashing," while right-leaning outlets de-emphasize details beyond the core plea, using stark terms like "ramming.

Center perspectives highlight granular elements such as the perpetrator's age of 36, five consecutive rams, and initial hate crime charges, introducing tones of concern via "attack" and "damaging," alongside religious freedom and mental health worries.
Pivotal divides emerge over charge histories and societal implications, which trigger expanded scrutiny in center views.
All agree on the guilty plea and intent to damage the Jewish landmark, underscoring divides in punitive versus contextual priorities amid cultural tensions over religious sites.
summary: On Wednesday, Dan Sohail, 36, of Carteret, New Jersey, pleaded guilty in Brooklyn federal court to intentionally damaging the Chabad-Lubavitch world headquarters by ramming his car into the building.

The January 28 attack occurred during the 75th anniversary of Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson's leadership; Sohail cleared stanchions and rammed his vehicle into the entrance five consecutive times, causing roughly $19,000 in damage.

Although facing a maximum three-year sentence, federal guidelines suggest Sohail could receive zero to six months in prison, as prosecutors did not pursue hate crime charges since intentionally damaging religious property is not categorized as such under federal law.

Chabad spokesperson Yaacov Behrman expressed disappointment at the potential short sentence, arguing it sends a message that attacking Jewish people is acceptable, while Justice Department civil rights division chief Harmeet Dhillon called the conduct a targeted attack on religious liberty.

Prosecutors acknowledged 'very significant mental health concerns' about Sohail at a March hearing, and the defendant remains jailed at the Metropolitan Detention Center in Brooklyn pending sentencing later this year.
title: Man Pleads Guilty to Ramming Car Into Chabad Lubavitch Headquarters in New York City

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Fani Willis 's "vows to sue" over a law "removing party labels" in Democratic-leaning Atlanta elections as righteous resistance to suppressive reform, heroizing her resolve with sympathetic tones, while right-leaning coverage portrays the same "vows" as aggressive overreach against a neutral "voting bill" aimed at integrity.
Pivotal divides emerge in de-emphasizing the law's scope—nonpartisan elections across five populous counties—by the right, versus left's focus on constitutional challenges.
Center-Leaning reports blend "threatens" and "stripping" with context on Democratic gains displacing Republicans.
All agree Gov.
Kemp signed the nonpartisan mandate and Willis's lawsuit intent, underscoring election fairness versus partisan entrenchment divides.
summary: Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp signed a law requiring nonpartisan elections for most local officials in the five most populous Atlanta-area counties, leading to threats of a lawsuit from two Democratic prosecutors over the bill's constitutionality.

Republicans have repeatedly targeted Fulton County District Attorney Fani Willis because of her prosecution of former Republican President Donald Trump after he pushed to overturn Democrat Joe Biden's win in Georgia in 2020.

The measure applies in Fulton, Clayton, Cobb, DeKalb and Gwinnett counties, with Democrats saying Republicans are trying to make it so GOP members running without party labels have a better chance to win in Democratic jurisdictions.
title: Fani Willis vows to sue over new Georgia law that removes party labels in Atlanta-area elections

bias: Left-Leaning outlets humanize the former mayor as "beloved" who "fears" deportation over a mere "voting controversy," evoking sympathy and portraying enforcement as harsh overreach, while right-leaning ones frame a "non-citizen" for "years of illegal voting" and illicit office-holding, emphasizing voter fraud accountability with stern tones.
Pivotal divides emerge in sympathy for his plight versus demands for deportation, with right amplifying violation duration absent on left.
Center coverage neutrally highlights "voted illegally," guilty pleas to three counts, and $2,000 fines.
All agree on his non-citizen status, admitted illegal voting, and ICE detention, underscoring clashes over enforcement rigor versus immigrant vulnerabilities.
summary: Former Coldwater, Kansas Mayor Joe Ceballos was detained by federal immigration authorities after admitting he voted in U.S. elections despite not being a citizen, according to his attorney.
Joe Ceballos, a legal permanent resident originally from Mexico, resigned as mayor while facing charges tied to noncitizen voting and now fears possible deportation.

Supporters gathered outside an ICE office in Wichita as former Kansas mayor Joe Ceballos reported for a meeting with immigration officials following his misdemeanor plea deal in an election-related case.
title: Immigration Authorities Detain Former Kansas Mayor Who Fears Deportation over Voting Controversy

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Denise Powell's primary win as a resilient triumph in Nebraska 's "blue dot" district, emphasizing "top battleground" status and Democratic flip potential with optimistic tones like "tough primary fight.

Right-Leaning coverage portrays it as a "contentious" intra-party squabble in retiring Rep. Don Bacon's territory, downplaying the victory by highlighting narrow margins like 38.9% to 36.8% and Republican Brinker Harding's matchup, using skeptical phrasing to underscore GOP resilience.
Center-Leaning reports neutrally note "razor-thin" leads and Cavanaugh's non-concession for balance.

Pivotal divides emerge over "blue dot" symbolism—celebrated leftward, anomalous rightward—yet all agree on Powell's AP-called victory in the close race, reflecting broader red-state electoral turf wars.
summary: Denise Powell won the Democratic primary in Nebraska 's 2nd District with 39% of the vote, narrowly defeating John Cavanaugh who had 37%.
Powell will face Republican Brinker Harding, an Omaha City Council member who ran unopposed for the Republican nomination, in the general election to replace retiring GOP Rep. Don Bacon.
Nebraska's 2nd District is a key swing seat that Democrats aim to flip, especially after the retirement of the incumbent representative.
title: Denise Powell wins Democratic primary in Nebraska’s ‘blue dot’ 2nd District

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the incident through sympathetic lenses like "shot dead" and "search of work ," emphasizing Palestinian desperation and implied excessive force, while right-leaning coverage highlights "climbing" the "barrier into Israel" or "separation wall," portraying it as a justified security response to illicit breach.
Emotional tones diverge sharply: left employs dramatic, victim-focused phrasing evoking outrage, right uses action-oriented terms suggesting provocation.
Pivotal divides emerge in the barrier's depiction—"occupied West Bank barrier" versus defensive "wall"—and right's mention of a second wounded Palestinian, de-emphasized elsewhere.
Center coverage adds neutral details like the victim's age and hometown but retains sympathetic echoes.
All agree on the man being shot while attempting the work-motivated crossing, underscoring ideological rifts over borders and security versus humanitarian motives.
summary: On Tuesday evening, Palestinian authorities reported that Israeli police shot and killed Zakaria Qatusa, 44, as he attempted to climb the concrete barrier separating the West Bank from Jerusalem near Al-Ram.
Brother Khalid Qatusa stated the father of 4 was attempting to enter Israel for work, saying he felt forced to resort to this method as there was no other opportunity to support his household.

Since Hamas-led militants attacked southern Israel on Oct. 7, 2023, access has been sharply restricted, driving increasing numbers of Palestinians from the West Bank to attempt illegal crossings for work in recent years.

Mourners gathered in the West Bank town of Deir Qadis on Wednesday for Qatusa's funeral, while separately, mourners in al-Lubban al-Sharqiya held services for Yousef Kaabnah, 16, killed by Israeli army fire.
title: Palestinian Man Shot Dead While Climbing West Bank Barrier Into Israel in Search of Work

bias: Left-Leaning coverage highlights 'zero' US aid and 'tax dollars', framing Netanyahu's stance as timely fiscal relief while emphasizing Gulf state ties, whereas right-leaning outlets stress 'must become independent' with urgent, patriotic tones portraying self-reliance as a bold imperative.

Left employs dramatic phrases like 'It’s Time' for sensational appeal, right evokes sovereignty strength; pivotal divides emerge on de-emphasized Iran misjudgments, which right and center underscore versus left's focus on aid weaning.
The $3.8 billion annual aid is left's taxpayer burden but right's path to autonomy.
Center sticks to neutral 'draw down to zero' phrasing.
All agree on decade-long reduction to reset ties, reflecting ideological rifts over entanglement versus nationalism.
summary: On Sunday, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu told CBS News' '60 Minutes' he plans to phase out U.S. military financial support over the next decade, stating 'I want to draw down to zero the American financial support' currently worth $3.8 billion annually.

Congressional opposition to Israeli military aid has intensified, with Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez vowing last month to oppose all funding, as public sentiment shifted dramatically following the Gaza war outbreak in October 2023.

Israel currently receives $3.8 billion annually under a $38 billion U.S. commitment through 2028, now facing heightened political scrutiny; a March Pew survey found 60% of U.S. adults hold an unfavorable view of Israel, up nearly 20 points in four years.

Netanyahu attributed declining American support to what he claimed foreign countries have 'basically manipulated' on social media, stating this 'correlates almost 100%' with reduced backing, though he opposes censorship. He said President Donald Trump 's reaction was notably surprised.
title: Netanyahu wants Israel "to draw down to zero the American financial support"

bias: Left-Leaning outlets emphasize aggressive destruction via "bulldozers raze" Palestinian shops for "settlement-linked" roads, highlighting Bedouin displacement and segregation plans to evoke victimhood, while right-leaning frames demolish industrial establishments amid "illegal settlement expansion," using "destroy" to critique expansionism and de-emphasizing unpermitted builds.
Left employs vivid, emotive imagery for outrage; right applies partisan labels like "illegal" for pointed condemnation.
Pivotal divides: humanitarian fallout versus economic impacts of 40-50 shops.
Center notes "tore down" structures without permits, adding rationale.
Both concur on al-Eizariya demolitions for settlement roads, underscoring ideological clashes over occupation.
summary: On Tuesday, Israeli bulldozers demolished dozens of Palestinian shops in Al-Eizariya, clearing land for a road project in the occupied West Bank.

COGAT stated the structures were built illegally and obstructed a planned road, though the Palestinian Authority argues the demolitions facilitate a broader plan to reroute traffic away from Israeli settlements.

Shop owner Mohammad Abu Ghalieh said, "Forty-eight years of night and day to build something for his children and himself, and in one day and one night, everything was gone." Daoud Jahalin reported more than 200 families would lose their incomes.
Attorneys appealed to the Supreme Court, but the demolitions proceeded; Peace Now director Hagit Ofran said the project aims to "divert all Palestinian traffic" to isolate the E1 area.

Critics warn the E1 project isolates Ramallah and Bethlehem, hindering north-south movement for Palestinians; the strategy creates separate road systems for Israelis and Palestinians, complicating efforts to establish a contiguous Palestinian state.
title: Israeli bulldozers raze about 50 Palestinian shops ahead of settlement-linked road project

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Massie as one of the party's "most independent voices" Trump is "trying to oust," employing "tests" on "loyal" voters to underscore GOP fractures, while right-leaning outlets prioritize Trump allegiance, wielding "Trump derangement syndrome" to skewer Massie's breaks over Iran war and Epstein issues.
Emotional tones diverge—left's sympathetic portrayal of independence versus right's accusatory tribalism—with pivotal rifts in rift details and Gallrein's quote, de-emphasized leftward.
Center neutrally sequences event speakers, omitting feuds.
All converge on the Covington rally testing dual loyalties, exposing conservatism's independence versus fealty divide.
summary: Rep. Thomas Massie defends his seat against Ed Gallrein, a challenger drafted by President Donald Trump, in Kentucky's 4th Congressional District primary next Tuesday.

Trump has frequently attacked Massie, calling him a "moron" and "nut job" who "will go down as the WORST Republican Congressman" for opposing tax legislation and pushing to release Jeffrey Epstein files.

At the Lincoln Day Dinner in Covington, voters expressed conflicting loyalties; retired officer Steve Jarvis decided to vote against Massie for the first time, while others praised his strong convictions.
Massie told The Associated Press that this primary is "by far the most challenging reelection I" have ever faced, particularly in a district that supported the president by 35 points two years ago.
Gallrein claims Massie suffers from "a severe case of Trump derangement syndrome," while Massie argues his stance on Iran aligns with America First promises made during the campaign.
title: Thomas Massie tests if voters can stay loyal to him and Donald Trump at the same time
